steps.title: In the Shop 🏪
steps.desc: Learn to shop like a local! Ask prices, try things on, and make purchases in Dutch.

steps.note: De-word. 'Winkelen' = to shop (verb). 'Winkelen gaan' = to go shopping.

steps.exampleEn: I want to buy these pants.
steps.note: Essential shopping verb! Past: gekocht. 'Inkopen' = groceries (noun).
Opposite of kopen. Past: verkocht. 'Te koop' = for sale.
'Kosten' (verb) vs 'de kosten' (the costs/expenses). Very regular verb.
De-word. 'Duur' = expensive. 'Goedkoop' = cheap. 'Prijzig' = pricey.
Also means 'duration'. Context tells you which! 'Duur' vs 'duurder' (more expensive).
Literally 'good-buy'! Positive meaning (unlike English 'cheap'). Opposite: duur.
'Betalen' = to pay. 'De betaling' = the payment. 'Ik betaal' = I pay.
'Passen' = to fit/try on. 'De paskamer' = fitting room. 'Het past (me)' = it fits (me).
De-word. 'Maat S/M/L' or numbers. 'Dezelfde maat' = the same size.

steps.exampleEn: How much does this sweater cost?
steps.note: PHRASE: Essential shopping question! 'Hoeveel kosten deze?' (plural). Response: '€10' or 'Tien euro'.

steps.title: 🗣️ Dialogue: Clothes Shopping
steps.text:
A: Goedemiddag! Kan ik u helpen?
B: Ja, ik zoek een jas.
A: Welke maat heeft u?
B: Maat M. Mag ik deze zwarte passen?
A: Natuurlijk! De paskamer is daar.
[Later]
B: Hij past perfect! Hoeveel kost hij?
A: €79,95.
B: Oké, ik neem hem.
A: Wilt u pinnen of contant betalen?
B: Pinnen, alstublieft.

Translation:
A: Good afternoon! Can I help you?
B: Yes, I'm looking for a coat.
A: What size are you?
B: Size M. May I try this black one on?
A: Of course! The fitting room is there.
[Later]
B: It fits perfectly! How much does it cost?
A: €79.95.
B: Okay, I'll take it.
A: Would you like to pay by card or cash?
B: Card, please.

steps.title: 🛍️ Shopping Phrases
steps.text:
ASKING:
Hoeveel kost dit/deze? — How much does this cost?
Mag ik dit passen? — May I try this on?
Heeft u dit in maat...? — Do you have this in size...?
Heeft u andere kleuren? — Do you have other colors?

RESPONDING:
Ik zoek... — I'm looking for...
Ik wil graag... — I would like...
Ik neem hem/haar/het — I'll take it
Dat is te duur — That's too expensive
Ik kijk even — I'm just looking

SIZES:
Maat S/M/L/XL — Size S/M/L/XL
Maat 38, 40, 42... — Size 38, 40, 42...
Een maatje groter/kleiner — One size bigger/smaller

steps.q: What does 'passen' mean in a shop?

steps.title: Money Talk 💶
steps.desc: Handle money like a pro! Learn about euros, paying methods, and getting change.

steps.note: De-word. Currency of Netherlands (and most EU). Symbol: €. Plural: euro (no -s!).
De-word. 100 cents = 1 euro. Often written: €5,20 (comma, not period!).

steps.en: to pay by card / to use a debit card
steps.note: Very Dutch! 'Pinnen' = pay by card. 'De pinpas' = debit card. 'De pinautomaat' = ATM.
'Contant betalen' = pay cash. 'Contant geld' = cash money. Dutch are going cashless!
Het-word. 'Wisselen' = to change/exchange. 'Wisselgeld terug' = change back.
De-word. 'Korting krijgen' = get discount. 'Met korting' = with discount.
Literally 'out-sale'. 'Alles moet weg!' = Everything must go! Summer/winter sales are huge.
De-word. From Italian 'cassa'. 'Kassa 3 is open' = Checkout 3 is open.
Het-word. Diminutive of 'bon'. 'Het kassabonnetje' = receipt. Always ask: 'Met of zonder bonnetje?'
De-word. 'PIN' = Personal Identification Number. Almost everyone pins in NL!

steps.title: 🗣️ Dialogue: At the Checkout
steps.text:
A: Dat is €24,50 alstublieft.
B: Kan ik pinnen?
A: Ja, natuurlijk.
[B pins]
A: Wilt u een bonnetje?
B: Ja, graag.
A: Alstublieft. Fijne dag!
B: U ook, dank u!

At a market:
A: Hoeveel is dit?
B: Drie euro vijftig.
A: Alstublieft. [Geeft €5]
B: En hier is uw wisselgeld: €1,50.
A: Dank u wel!

Translation:
A: That's €24.50 please.
B: Can I pay by card?
A: Yes, of course.
[B pays by card]
A: Would you like a receipt?
B: Yes, please.
A: Here you go. Have a nice day!
B: You too, thank you!

At a market:
A: How much is this?
B: Three fifty.
A: Here you go. [Gives €5]
B: And here's your change: €1.50.
A: Thank you!

steps.title: 💶 Money in the Netherlands
steps.text:
PAYMENT CULTURE:
💳 95% of transactions are by card
💵 Cash is disappearing fast
📱 Tikkie app for splitting bills
🏧 ATMs everywhere (geldautomaat)

SAYING PRICES:
€5 — vijf euro
€5,50 — vijf euro vijftig / vijf vijftig
€10,95 — tien euro vijfennegentig / tien vijfennegentig
€0,50 — vijftig cent

WRITING PRICES:
Dutch uses comma: €10,50 (not €10.50)
Thousands: €1.000 (not €1,000)

COMMON PHRASES:
Kan ik pinnen? — Can I pay by card?
Neemt u contant aan? — Do you accept cash?
Met of zonder bonnetje? — With or without receipt?
Dat is €... — That's €...

steps.s: Dat kost vijf euro en twintig _____.

steps.q: How do you write €10.50 in Dutch?

steps.title: At the Market 🥕
steps.desc: Experience Dutch market culture! Buy fresh produce, ask for quantities, and haggle (a little).

steps.note: De-word. Saturday markets are huge in NL! 'De weekmarkt' = weekly market.
'Vers' = fresh (for food). 'Versgebakken brood' = freshly baked bread. Very important at markets!

steps.example: Ik koop groente op de markt.
steps.exampleEn: I buy vegetables at the market.
steps.note: De-word. Usually plural: 'groenten'. 'De groenteboer' = vegetable vendor.
Het-word. From French. Collective noun (like English). 'De fruitboer' = fruit vendor.
Weight measurement. 'Een halve kilo' = half a kilo. 'Twee kilo' (no -s!).
Dutch pound = 500g (not British/American pound!). 'Een half pond' = 250g.
'Proeven' = to taste/try. At markets: always ask 'Mag ik proeven?' Very normal!
De-word. NL is famous for cheese! Gouda, Edam, Maasdam. Markets have kaas stands.
De-word. 'Bloemen' = flowers (plural). Flower markets are iconic! 'Een bosje' = small bouquet.

steps.en: What would you like? / What can I get you?
steps.note: PHRASE: Classic vendor greeting! Shows politeness. Literally: 'What may it be?'

steps.title: 🗣️ Dialogue: At the Market
steps.text:
A: Goedemorgen! Wat mag het zijn?
B: Goedemorgen! Een kilo tomaten, alstublieft.
A: Verse tomaten vandaag! En verder?
B: Mag ik die kaas proeven?
A: Natuurlijk! [Geeft stukje]
B: Hmmm, lekker! Een pond graag.
A: Prima. Is dat alles?
B: Ja, dat is alles. Hoeveel is het?
A: Dat is €6,50 bij elkaar.
B: Alstublieft.
A: Dank u! Fijne dag!

Translation:
A: Good morning! What would you like?
B: Good morning! A kilo of tomatoes, please.
A: Fresh tomatoes today! And anything else?
B: May I taste that cheese?
A: Of course! [Gives piece]
B: Mmm, delicious! A pound please.
A: Great. Is that everything?
B: Yes, that's all. How much is it?
A: That's €6.50 altogether.
B: Here you go.
A: Thank you! Have a nice day!

steps.title: 🥕 Dutch Market Culture
steps.text:
MARKET FACTS:
📅 Most towns have weekly markets
🕐 Usually Wednesday or Saturday
🥬 Best prices on produce
🧀 Try before you buy (cheese!)
🌷 Flower stalls everywhere

QUANTITIES:
een kilo — 1 kg
een pond — 500g (Dutch pound)
een half pond — 250g
een ons — 100g (old measure, rare)
een stuk — one piece
een bosje — a bunch

MARKET VOCABULARY:
de kraam — the stall
de marktkraam — market stall
de koopman — the vendor
vers — fresh
biologisch — organic
seizoen — in season

FAMOUS MARKETS:
Albert Cuypmarkt (Amsterdam)
Dappermarkt (Amsterdam)
Blaak Market (Rotterdam)

TIP: Markets are 30-50% cheaper than supermarkets!

steps.title: 🛍️ Shopping Summary
steps.text:
SHOPPING PHRASES:
Hoeveel kost dit/deze? — How much?
Mag ik passen? — May I try on?
Ik neem hem/haar/het — I'll take it
Kan ik pinnen? — Can I pay by card?
Wat mag het zijn? — What would you like?

MONEY:
de euro — euro
de cent — cent
pinnen — pay by card
contant — cash
het wisselgeld — change
de korting — discount

QUANTITIES:
een kilo — 1 kg
een pond — 500g
een stuk — one piece

LOCATIONS:
de winkel — shop
de supermarkt — supermarket
de markt — market
de kassa — checkout

Dutch shopping culture: mostly cashless, markets on weekends, try cheese before buying!
